coredata.dc:title: Quasi-solid, bio-renewable supercapacitor with high specific capacitance and energy density based on rice electrolytes and rice straw-derived carbon dots as novel electrolyte additives
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Jorn-am, Thanapat
Praneerad, Janjira
Attajak, Rodsathon
Sirisit, Natee
Manyam, Jedsada
Paoprasert, Peerasak
coredata.dc:description: In this work, novel quasi-solid electrolytes and carbon dots as additives were developed for the bio-renewable, high energy-density supercapacitor. The activated carbon from rice straw was used to make an electrode while the carbon dots also from rice straw were combined with rice electrolytes. The supercapacitor using H3PO4/rice electrolyte yielded a specific capacitance, potential window, and energy density of 88 F g−1, 2 V, and 12 Wh kg−1, respectively, at a scan rate of 30 mV s−1. For comparison, electrolyte prepared from conventional H3PO4/poly(vinyl alcohol) yielded only 45 F g−1, 1.6 V, and 4 Wh kg−1. The addition of carbon dots to H3PO4/rice electrolytes further increased the specific capacitance, potential window, and energy density to 144 F g−1, 2.4 V, and 29 Wh kg−1, equivalent to 163%, 120%, and 241% enhancement, respectively. The maximum specific capacitance and energy density reached 491 F g−1 and 98 Wh kg−1, respectively, at a scan rate of 2 mV s−1. The carbon dots improved the supercapacitor performance by simultaneously enhancing the adsorption and diffusion of electroactive species and surface capacitance. Interestingly, the electrode was prepared without the addition of highly conductive carbon and still produced excellent supercapacitor performances. This work thus demonstrates a novel and simple strategy for using a bio-renewable resource for the fabrication of high-performance supercapacitors, which have great practical potential in the field of portable and wearable electronic devices.

head.ce:title: Quasi-solid, bio-renewable supercapacitor with high specific capacitance and energy density based on rice electrolytes and rice straw-derived carbon dots as novel electrolyte additives

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, novel quasi-solid electrolytes and carbon dots as additives were developed for the bio-renewable, high energy-density supercapacitor. The activated carbon from rice straw was used to make an electrode while the carbon dots also from rice straw were combined with rice electrolytes. The supercapacitor using H3PO4/rice electrolyte yielded a specific capacitance, potential window, and energy density of 88 F g−1, 2 V, and 12 Wh kg−1, respectively, at a scan rate of 30 mV s−1. For comparison, electrolyte prepared from conventional H3PO4/poly(vinyl alcohol) yielded only 45 F g−1, 1.6 V, and 4 Wh kg−1. The addition of carbon dots to H3PO4/rice electrolytes further increased the specific capacitance, potential window, and energy density to 144 F g−1, 2.4 V, and 29 Wh kg−1, equivalent to 163%, 120%, and 241% enhancement, respectively. The maximum specific capacitance and energy density reached 491 F g−1 and 98 Wh kg−1, respectively, at a scan rate of 2 mV s−1. The carbon dots improved the supercapacitor performance by simultaneously enhancing the adsorption and diffusion of electroactive species and surface capacitance. Interestingly, the electrode was prepared without the addition of highly conductive carbon and still produced excellent supercapacitor performances. This work thus demonstrates a novel and simple strategy for using a bio-renewable resource for the fabrication of high-performance supercapacitors, which have great practical potential in the field of portable and wearable electronic devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The raw data required to reproduce these findings will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, energy demand is rapidly increasing, raising concerns over energy shortages. Energy, produced from water, wind, solar, and biomass, is thus considered renewable and has recently been replacing petroleum or fossil fuel energy. The production of renewable energy requires an energy storage device so that the energy can be stored for use at any place and time. Supercapacitors are widely regarded as promising energy storage devices because of their high power density, fast charge-discharge rate, stability, and long lifetime [1,2]. Supercapacitors store charges through electrostatic interaction at the interface between electrode and electrolyte [3]. Carbon-based materials, especially activated carbon, are popular choices as electrode materials in electric double layer (EDL) supercapacitors due to their high porosity, chemical and physical stability, low cost, and convenience of production from renewable sources [4–6]. However, the specific capacitance of activated carbon electrodes is still inferior to many other types of materials, such as conjugated polymers and inorganic oxides, which generally function as pseudocapacitive materials [7]. The electrical performance of carbon-based supercapacitors needs to be improved in order to be practical. This can be achieved by the fabrication of carbon-based materials with high surface area and suitable pore sizes and the introduction of pseudocapacitive properties.

Despite the development of highly porous carbon-based materials and methods for introducing the pseudocapacitance, the carbon-based supercapacitors still suffer from much lower energy density than their battery counterparts [8]. The low energy density can be effectively overcome by enlarging the potential window in addition to increasing the specific capacitance. To enlarge the potential window, aqueous electrolytes must be avoided because the potential window of water is only 1.2 V [9]. Solid electrolytes eliminate the use of water, but suffer from poor ionic conductivity, subsequently leading to low specific capacitance [10,11]. Alternative choices are organic and quasi-solid or polymer-based electrolytes. Organic-based electrolytes suffer from volatility and leakage problems and their performance become worse when moisture is present [12]. Therefore, the quasi-solid electrolytes are better choices in terms of practicality, safety, and cost. In addition to high potential windows, they also offer better device chemical and physical stability and lifetime because the presence of volatile solvent is minimal. Although water is commonly used as a solvent in quasi-solid electrolytes, their potential windows are generally wider than those of the aqueous electrolytes due to the stabilizing effects through solvation and formation of three-dimensional polymeric networks, which can trap water in the polymer matrices via surface tension and intermolecular interactions [13]. Most common quasi-solid electrolytes include ion-conducting polymers, such as poly(ethylene oxide) [14–16], poly(vinyl alcohol) [17–22], poly(acrylic acid), potassium polyacrylate, poly(methyl methacrylate), poly(ether ether ketone), etc [9]. However, these are synthetic polymers prepared from non-renewable petroleum-based precursors. The use of renewable materials as quasi-solid electrolytes is therefore attractive from a practical point of view.

In this work, the electrode and quasi-solid electrolyte in supercapacitors were prepared from rice straw and rice, respectively. Rice (Oryza sativa) is one of the most abundant food crops in the world and the main source of carbohydrates along with proteins and other essential nutrients. Roughly, 800–1000 million tons per year of rice straw are produced globally with about 600–800 million tons per year produced in Asia alone. The production of rice leads to a large amount of rice straw. Rice straw is often discarded or burnt, causing severe environmental problems and global warming. Rice straw is a lignocellulosic biomass, mainly consisting of lignin, cellulose, and hemicellulose which are rich in carbon. Therefore, the use of rice straw as a raw material for the synthesis of carbon materials will create value and reduce environmental problems. For an electrolyte, rice was used as the ion host in this work. Rice as a biopolymer is a polysaccharide, consisting of glucose molecules as repeating units that can solvate ions. Carbohydrate-based biopolymers have received considerable attention in supercapacitor applications due to their environmentally friendliness, nontoxicity, abundance, and low-price. Those such as carrageenan [23], chitosan [24], and cellulose [25] have shown remarkable properties as electrolytes in electronic devices. For example, Mohammad et al. obtained high ionic conductivity from solid polymer electrolyte samples using rice starch, sodium iodide, and 1-methyl-3-propylimidazolium iodide ionic liquid for dye-sensitized solar cells [26]. Moshuqi et al. obtained excellent supercapacitor recyclability using cellulose-based electrolyte [27]. Therefore, as a highly solvating and bio-renewable carbohydrate-based polymer, rice has potential to be used in quasi-solid electrolytes of supercapacitors for large-scale applications, but have never been investigated.

Here, novel supercapacitors were fabricated from rice and rice straw. Specifically, activated carbon and carbon dots were prepared from rice straw while rice was used for the preparation of electrolytes ( Fig. 1). For the first time, the carbon dots were used as additives in gel electrolytes for enhancing the supercapacitor performance. Carbon dots are nanoparticles with unique properties such as size in nanometer, high surface area, diverse functional groups and heteroatoms on their surface area, and structural tunability [28–30]. They can be prepared from cheap, readily available precursors using straightforward synthesis methods. Carbon dots are rich in oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface formed during the synthesis of carbon dots, which normally involves the use of oxidation reaction of precursors in acidic solutions [31–33]. The presence of oxygen and nitrogen atoms are known to enhance hydrophilicity, ion adsorption, surface wettability, facilitation of charge transport, electrochemical conductivity, electron transfer, and can act as electron reservoirs [34]. Furthermore, they can introduce pseudo-capacitive behaviors and favorable contact between electrodes and electrolytes and therefore have been used as additives in supercapacitor electrodes [12]. However, to the best of our knowledge the use of carbon dots in supercapacitor gel electrolytes has never been reported. Thus, the novel use of carbon dots as electrolyte additives will lead to further development of high-performance electrolytes, which are also the key components in supercapacitors. In addition, the fabrication of novel high-performance supercapacitors from bio-renewable rice and its waste developed in this work will benefit the large-scale, practical production of efficient energy storage systems. This work also demonstrates a proof-of-concept that many other abundant crops could be turned into efficient, low-cost energy storage devices. A high energy-density energy storage device fabricated from readily available resources is highly desirable for large-scale, low-cost electronic applications.

Rice straw was obtained from a local rice paddy field in Pathumthani, Thailand and rice was obtained from a local supermarket. All chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and were of analytical grade. Cellulose dialysis membrane (MWCO = 1 kDa) was purchased from Spectrum Labs. Nickel foam sheets (1 µM thick) were purchased from a local supplier in Thailand. Deionized water was used in all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of rice straw activated carbon (RSAC) and carbon dots (CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Rice straw was washed with plenty of water, sun-dried for 36 h, cut, and crushed into small pieces. The crushed, dried rice straw (5 g) was mixed with 1.5 M phosphoric acid solution (100 mL) and ultrasonicated for 15 min. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 5 h. After the reaction was completed, the reaction mixture was cooled down to room temperature, ultrasonicated for 15 min, and filtered to separate carbon char and filtrate. The filtrate was kept for the extraction of CDs. The carbon char was washed, dried and crushed to a fine powder form. The carbon char (3 g) was mixed with KOH as a chemical activating agent in the presence of deionized water (60 mL) and then heated to 90 °C for 1 h to form a slurry. The slurry was pyrolyzed with a constant flow of N2 gas. The resultant material was washed with hot deionized water and 1 M HCl until becoming neutral and dried for 24 h in an oven at 80 °C to obtain activated carbon. The KOH/carbon char weight ratio, activation temperature, and activation time varied as summarized in Table S1.

The filtrate was neutralized with 10 M NaOH solution. The solution was purified using a dialysis membrane (MWCO = 1 kDa) for 36 h in deionized water, deionized water was replaced every 6 h and then freeze-dried to yield the CDs as brown solid powder (1.13 g, 22.7%).

Activated carbon and poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF) as a binder were mixed in N-methylpyrrolidone (NMP) in a weight ratio of 95:5 w/w to form a slurry. The slurry was coated on a piece of nickel foam sheet and dried at 80 °C for 1 h prior to characterizations. The electrode was then immersed into an electrolyte for 24 h prior to electrical characterizations. The mass loading of activated carbon on electrodes is in between 3 and 5 mg.

Rice grains (10 g) were added to deionized water (100 mL) in a beaker and the mixture was heated at 100 °C under continuous stirring for 30 min until becoming a viscous mixture and then a concentrated phosphoric acid (5, 10, 15, 20, and 25 g) was added with continuous stirring for 30 min to obtain homogeneous H3PO4/rice electrolyte. For the carbon dot-added electrolytes, CDs (20, 60, and 100 mg) were added to the homogenous H3PO4/rice mixture, which was then stirred for an additional 30 min

As a standard electrolyte, H3PO4/poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) electrolyte was prepared. PVA (10 g) was added to deionized water (100 mL) in a beaker and was continuously stirred for 1 h until a clear solution of PVA was obtained. Then 10 g of H3PO4 was added to the PVA solution with continuous stirring for 30 min to obtain a H3PO4/PVA electrolyte.

The surface morphology of activated carbon was analyzed using a scanning electron microscope (Hitachi, S-3400). The samples were mounted on a carbon tape. Specific surface area, pore size, and pore volume were measured using nitrogen adsorption-desorption (Quantachrome, Autosorb iQ-C). The Brunauer-Emmett- Teller (BET) method was adopted to calculate the specific surface area and the Barret-Joner-Halenda (BJH) method was used to calculate the pore volume and pore size distribution. The crystallinity of activated carbon was characterized using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku, SmartLab) and Raman spectrometer (HORIBA, MacroRAM). The UV–visible absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of CDs were measured using a UV–visible spectrometer (Shimadzu, UV-1700 PharmaSpec) and fluorescence emission spectrometer (Jasco, FP-6200), respectively. The Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra of CDs was measured using an FT-IR spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, Spectrum 2000) and using KBr powder pressed pellets. An X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (AXIS ULTRADLD, Kratos Analytical) was used to characterize the element surface and compositions of CDs. A transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of CDs was investigated using an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 microscope. The zeta potential of carbon dot solution was measured using a Nano Particle Analyzer (HORIBA, Nano Partica SZ-100) at 25 °C with a scattering angle of 173°.

The electrochemical characterizations were performed using a potentiostat/galvanostat (μAutolab, PGSTAT204). Two types of electrochemical cells were used: (a) a three-electrode cell containing activated carbon/PVDF as a working electrode, platinum wire as a counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl reference electrode and (b) a two-electrode cell containing a pair of symmetric activated carbon/PVDF electrodes. The three-electrode cell was characterized using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The EIS was conducted in the frequency range between 100 kHz and 100 mHz with an amplitude of 10 mV. The two-electrode cell was characterized using galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) measurement. The CV in PVA electrolyte was carried out at the potential range of −0.4–1.2 V at various scan rates from 10 to 100 mV s−1. The CV and GCD experiments using rice electrolyte were carried out at the potential range of −0.7–1.7 V at various scan rates from 10 to 100 mV s−1 and current densities ranging from 0.8 to 5 A g−1, respectively. A Swagelok cell was utilized to measure the cyclability using Whatman filter paper and nickel foam with a diameter of 1 cm as separator and current collector, respectively. The specific capacitance (C

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of activated carbon
ce:section.ce:para: Rice straw is a common agricultural waste which is rich in carbon due to the presence of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin. In this work, a two-in-one approach was used for preparing both activated carbon and CDs from rice straw. After hydrothermal treatment of the rice straw, the carbon char and CDs were separated by simple gravitational filtration. The carbon char was proceeded for the preparation of activated carbon and then the electrode whereas the CDs were used as additives in electrolyte, which will be discussed later. The SEM image of the carbon char was taken of and compared with that of rice straw ( Fig. 2a and b). In addition to the different physical appearance of the carbon char as black powder as opposed to the brown straw, the SEM image of the carbon char looked very different from that of the untreated rice straw. The rice straw showed a rather smooth surface composed of fibril bundles covered under the epidermis [35,36] while the carbon char instead showed large spherical particles with a size of over 2 µM. This result suggests that the hydrothermal treatment successfully converted the rice straw to carbon char. In the presence of acid and under high temperature and pressure, the rice straw was transformed into the carbon char via hydrolysis and carbonization processes [37]. The char was then mixed with KOH and then thermally activated with a continuous flow of N2 gas. KOH was chosen as a chemical activating agent because it is widely used for producing high-quality activated carbon with enhanced microporosity as well as mesostructural order [38]. Under high temperature, KOH was converted to potassium compounds, such as K2O and K2CO3, which then penetrated and etched the carbon framework of char and created the pore network within the char [39–41]. After the removal of potassium compounds, the expanded and etched carbon structure was preserved, resulting in a highly porous material, so called activated carbon [42]. Seven activating conditions were applied to investigate the effects of KOH/carbon char weight ratio, activation temperature, and activation time on the structural and electrical properties of the activated carbon (Table S1). After washing and drying, the activated carbon was obtained.

The effects of the KOH/carbon char weight ratio (0.5:1, 1:1, and 1:0.5 for RSAC1, RSAC2, and RSAC3, respectively) were investigated first. After chemical activation of carbon char, all three samples showed cracked and pitted morphology (Fig. 2). However, RSAC1 and RSAC3 showed a variety of particle sizes and irregular particles whereas RSAC2 showed smaller and more regular particle sizes. RSAC1 yielded much lower surface area and pore volume, but larger pore size, compared to RSAC2 and RSAC3 (Table S2), indicating that the concentration of KOH was insufficient to produce microporous structures in RSAC1 [43]. This means the weight ratio between KOH and carbon char is important in controlling the morphology and porosity of the activated carbon.

Next, the effects of the activation temperature (700, 800, and 900 °C for RSAC4, RSAC2, and RSAC5) were investigated. The SEM image of RSAC4 showed the disappearance of particles. However, RSAC2 showed small and regular particle sizes whereas RSAC5 exhibited larger rod and spherical-like particles. All three samples showed similar surface area; however, RSAC2 had a higher pore volume and lower pore size, compared to RSAC4 and RSAC5. This indicates that RSAC2 is more porous than RSAC4 and RSAC5 and the activation temperature governed the morphology and porosity of the activated carbon. The highest mass yield of RSAC4 also suggests an incomplete activation at 700 °C. Lastly, the effects of activation time (60, 90, and 120 min for RSAC2, RSAC6, and RSAC7) were examined. It was found that based on SEM images, increasing the activation time from 60 to 90 min resulted in the formation of larger particles. A further increase in activation time to 120 min did not significantly affect the morphology. From BET analysis, RSAC2 showed a larger surface area than RSAC6 and RSAC7. Long activation time might have destroyed the porous structure of the activated carbon [44]. Based on all of these results, the KOH/carbon char weight ratio, activation temperature, and activation time all affected the morphology and porosity of the resulting activated carbon.

The crystallinity of RSACs was further characterized. The XRD spectra of RSAC samples exhibited two broad diffraction peaks at θ = 24° and 43°, corresponding to the diffraction of (002) and (100) lattice planes, respectively ( Fig. 3a). Despite a highly amorphous structure, the more pronounced peak at 24° of RSAC2 as compared to those of other RSACs indicated a higher crystallinity in RSAC2. The Raman scattering spectra of all RSAC samples showed D band (1310 cm−1) and G band (1590 cm−1), representing the disordered and graphitic carbon structures, respectively (Fig. 3b). The ratio between ID and IG of RSAC2 is the lowest, signifying the highest content of graphitic structure and consistent with the highest crystallinity observed from the XRD results.

The electrochemical characterization of rice straw-derived activated carbon was investigated using a potentiostat/galvanostat. The cyclic voltammograms of RSAC1–7 were measured in conventional H3PO4/PVA electrolyte ( Fig. 4). It was found that RSAC2 had the highest specific capacitance of 30 F g−1 and a potential window of 1.6 V. According to the SEM, BET, XRD, and Raman results, it can be concluded that a combination of large surface area, large pore volume, small pore size, high crystallinity, and graphitic structure allowed RSAC2 to accommodate electrolytes, transport ions, and provide high electrical conductivity, giving rise to its highest charge storage [45]. RSAC4, however, yielded the lowest specific capacitance, which is consistent with the SEM image that its particulate structure formation was not completed. Based on its highest specific capacitance, RSAC2 was then chosen for subsequent experiments throughout the paper.

After separating CDs from carbon char, the CDs were purified by neutralization, dialysis, and freeze-drying to obtain the brown solid powder. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were used to identify the functional groups and chemical composition of CDs. The FT-IR spectrum of CDs in a range of 400–4000 cm−1 showed signals at 3451 cm−1, 2968 cm−1, 1631 cm−1, and 1092 cm−1, corresponding to N-H/O-H stretching, C-H stretching, CO/CC stretching, and C-O/Si-O stretching, respectively ( Fig. 5a). The survey XPS spectrum of CDs (Fig. 5b) showed major signals at 532.6 eV, 400.6 eV, 285.6 eV, and 103.6 eV, which were attributed to O1s, N1s C1s, and Si2p, with atomic concentrations of 47.19%, 2.15%, 31.90%, and 13.90%, respectively. The presence of the Si2p peak is due to the fact that silica is rich in cell walls of rice straw [32]. The high resolution of C1s spectrum (Fig. 5c) exhibited signals of C–N, CO, C-O, and CC at 289.2 eV, 288.2 eV, 286.5 eV and 285.1 eV, respectively. The high resolution of N1 s spectrum showed pyridinic, pyrrolic, graphitic, and oxidized nitrogen at 398.7 eV, 399.9 eV, 400.7 eV, and 402.2 eV, respectively (Fig. 5d). The high resolution of O1 s spectrum displayed signals of C–O–C, OC–O, C–O, and CO/ Si–O at 535.3 eV, 533.4 eV, 532.5 eV, and 531.1 eV, respectively [46,47] (Fig. 5e). Both FT-IR and XPS results confirmed the presence of nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional groups and expected elemental compositions in CDs. These results also confirm the hydrophilicity and stable dispersion of CDs in aqueous solution.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to characterize the morphology and size of CDs. The CDs had spherical shapes ( Fig. 6a) and the inset figure showed a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, corresponding to the (100) lattice plane of graphite. The CDs had diameters ranging from about 8–11 nm and approximately a diameter of 9 nm on average (Fig. 6b). The zeta potential of the CDs was determined to be −35.6 mV (Fig. S1), indicating the negatively charged functional groups on the surface and the stability of CDs.

UV–visible spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy were used to characterize the optical properties of CDs. The UV–visible spectrum of CDs (Fig. 6c) shows the absorption band ranging from 200 to 600 nm. Small peaks at 250 and 320 nm corresponded to the π -
π
*
transition of the aromatic sp2 and n-
π
*
transition of the CO bonds, respectively [48–50]. The fluorescence spectra with excitation wavelengths from 320 to 500 nm were obtained. An excitation wavelength of 345 nm showed a maximum emission intensity at an emission wavelength of 440 nm. The fluorescence spectra display excitation-dependent characteristics, attributed to the distribution of particle sizes and various emissive sites of CDs. Fluorescence emission intensity of CDs at an excitation wavelength of 345 nm was investigated from pH 1–13. It was found that fluorescence emission of CDs was not significantly altered by pH (Fig. 6d). The photostability of CDs was next investigated by measuring the UV–vis absorbance and fluorescence emission under 365-nm UV irradiation and ambient conditions (Fig. 6e and f). It was found that the photostability of the CDs was relatively stable under both UV irradiation for at least 8 h and ambient conditions for at least 8 weeks. This indicates that the CDs are stable for long-term use and application.

For a high energy-density supercapacitor, not only specific capacitance but also the potential window is important. In this study, the electrolyte was fabricated from rice and H3PO4 and compared to H3PO4/PVA electrolyte and the electrode was made from RSAC2. Upon mixing, the acidified rice became gel-like and highly viscous due to the solvation effects between ionic species and polysaccharides. The electrical properties were measured using cyclic voltammetry in a three-electrode system. As shown in Fig. 7(a), the cyclic voltammogram from H3PO4/rice electrolyte was slightly larger than from H3PO4/PVA electrolyte, giving a specific capacitance of 56 vs 45 F g−1, which is equivalent to a 124% enhancement. The H3PO4/rice electrolyte exhibited a 2.0 V potential window, which is higher than the H3PO4/PVA electrolyte by 0.4 V. The corresponding energy density of H3PO4/rice and H3PO4/PVA electrolytes are 8 and 4 Wh kg−1, equivalent to a 200% increase. As a result, the H3PO4/rice electrolyte is a promising alternative polymer electrolyte as it gave better electrical performance and is cheaper than the PVA, a conventional polymer for electrolyte. The highly solvating properties of rice led to the enhanced performance.

Next, to investigate the effects of H3PO4, the ratio between H3PO4 and rice was varied. Using 0.5:1 H3PO4/rice, the potential window, specific capacitance, and energy density of 1.9 V, 52 F g−1, and 7 Wh kg−1 (Fig. 7b and Table S3) were obtained. Upon increasing the ratio to 2.5:1 H3PO4/rice, the potential window and specific capacitance increased to 2.0 V and 109 F g−1, equivalent to an energy density of 15 Wh kg−1. This suggests that an improvement of electrical properties is due to an increase in ionic conductivity of the electrolyte. Nonetheless, all tested concentrations of H3PO4/rice even at 0.5:1 provided better electrical performance than the H3PO4/PVA electrolyte. For subsequent experiments, the 2:1 H3PO4/rice electrolyte was used because it produced excellent electrical properties and still had a quasi-solid appearance.

The CV and GCD of RSAC2 in 2:1 H3PO4/rice electrolyte were measured at scan rates from 10 mV s−1 to 100 mV s−1 and applied current densities from 0.8 A g−1 to 3 A g−1, respectively. The CV profile has quasi-rectangular shape, corresponding to the contribution from pseudocapacitance. The peak current was found to increase when the scan rate was increased (Fig. 7c). However, increasing the scan rate decreased the specific capacitance from 162 to 30 F g−1. The scan rate-dependence capacitance confirmed the diffusion-controlled process of electrolyte. The capacitance of the supercapacitor is enhanced when electrolyte can effectively diffuse through the micro and mesoporous surfaces. A low scan rate allows the electrolyte to diffuse through the micro and mesoporous surfaces, and therefore, the specific capacitance will be significantly enhanced. On the other hand, a high scan rate allows the electrolyte to access only the mesoporous surface, and specific capacitance is therefore suppressed [51]. The GCD profile also showed consistent results with CV results, in which increasing the current rate decreased specific capacitance (Fig. 7d). The GCD curves, especially at high current rates, were almost symmetrical with similar charge and discharge times, indicating a reversible charge-discharge cycle.

It has been reported that an addition of some redox active species or electro-active molecules to electrolytes can enhance the overall electrical performance [52]. CDs have been reported to enhance performance of various electronic and optoelectronic applications, including solar cells [53,54], sensors [55–57], batteries [58,59], light-emitting diodes [60,61], fuel cells [62], photocatalysts [63], etc. The use of CDs in supercapacitor electrodes has been also found to enhance the performance [64]. However, their uses as additives in supercapacitor electrolytes have never been reported. In this experiment, the CDs were then added to the H3PO4/rice electrolytes. Fig. 8(a) shows cyclic voltammograms of H3PO4/rice electrolyte and H3PO4/rice electrolytes in the presence of CDs at different concentrations. The 2:1 H3PO4/rice electrolyte provides a potential window, specific capacitance, and energy density of 2.0 V, 88 F g−1, and 12 Wh kg−1, respectively. In contrast, all H3PO4/rice electrolytes in the presence of CDs concentrations give 2.4 V ( Table 1). The widen potential window is due to the solvating effects of water molecules provided by polysaccharides in rice and the negatively charged CDs as confirmed by the zeta potential, which reduced the adverse hydrolysis reaction at both low and high potentials [65,66]. The specific capacitance and energy density of H3PO4/rice electrolyte with the presence of 0.02%wt CDs are 128 F g−1 and 26 Wh kg−1, respectively and further increased to 144 F g−1 and 29 Wh kg−1, 2.4 times higher than the H3PO4/rice electrolyte, when using 0.05%wt CDs. We also believed that with the surface negative charges, the CDs can act as the electrolytic ions and also ion-transport facilitators enhancing the ionic conductivity of the electrolytes [67]. This was confirmed by the increase in current response in the CV curves when the CDs were added. However, when increasing the concentration of CDs to 0.08%wt, the specific capacitance dropped, but the energy density was still higher than the pristine H3PO4/rice electrolyte. Too much amount of CDs in the electrolyte possibly caused an aggregation of ionic species, which decreased the specific capacitance of supercapacitors [68,69]. The current response is again a scan rate dependence (Fig. 8b and c). The highest specific capacitance of 491 F g−1 was obtained at the scan rate at 2 mV s−1. We further investigated the effects of other types of carbon materials as additives in H3PO4/rice electrolyte, including our rice straw-derived activated carbon (AC) and carbon black (CB). Unlike the CDs, AC and even highly conductive CB did not provide a significant improvement of specific capacitance and energy density as compared to the pristine H3PO4/rice electrolyte (Fig. 8d and Table 1). This suggests the contribution of small size, abundant functional groups, surface charges, and hydrophilicity of CDs to the improvement in supercapacitor performance.

The Nyquist plots of the supercapacitors were obtained (Fig. 8e). The series resistances of RSAC2 electrode in H3PO4/rice electrolyte, H3PO4/rice electrolyte with CDs, AC, and CB are 12.6, 7.7, 9.5, and 11.4 Ω, respectively, indicating all of the carbon materials reduced the series resistance when compared with the original H3PO4/rice electrolyte. It is obvious that the series resistance was the lowest when adding CDs to the electrolyte, which explains the highest specific capacitance obtained from the CD-containing electrolyte. Furthermore, at a high frequency region, a nearly vertical line of the CD-containing electrolyte indicates good EDL properties. Upon bending from 0° to 180°, the specific capacitances retained appreciably well (Fig. 8f and g). This result confirmed good electrical contacts within the electrode and between electrode and electrolyte and indicates the potential of our supercapacitors for flexible and wearable applications. As shown in Fig. 8h and Table 2, the highest specific capacitance reached 491 F g−1 and 98 Wh kg−1 at a scan rate of 2 mV s−1. It is clear that the energy density of our supercapacitor of 98 Wh kg−1 is the highest among other reported rice straw-derived supercapacitors. It is also on a par with battery counterparts [2]. This is due to a combination of a wide potential window and high specific capacitance. Comparing with supercapacitors fabricated from activated carbon derived from rice straw or those that used polysaccharide-based electrolytes in previous reports, the obtained energy density of our supercapacitor is the highest (Table 2). Moreover, the previously reported rice straw-derived supercapacitors involved the use of expensive ionic liquids, such as [EMIM][BF4] [70], strong acids, and strong bases, such as H2SO4 [71] and KOH [72], which are more corrosive and non-eco-friendly as compared to our H3PO4. Comparing with other starch-based electrolytes reported previously, such as neem gum [73], carboxymethylcellulose [23], and methylcellulose [74], our rice electrolyte is cheaper and more abundant. With a combination of high performance, low cost, and environmental friendliness, our supercapacitors are therefore more practical for large-scale production, which will benefit commercial, mainstream electronic devices. It is noteworthy to point out that our electrode was prepared from only activated carbon and PVDF binder without an addition of highly conductive carbon like carbon black or acetylene black, which were generally used for the previously reported supercapacitors.

To further investigate the effects of the CDs on the enhanced specific capacitance, the electron transfer process was analyzed from the CV profiles. Two systems, H3PO4/rice and H3PO4/rice/0.05%wt CDs electrolytes, were examined. The Cottrell’s equations (Eqs. 5 and 6) can be used to provide an information on the electron transfer process whether it is the diffusion-controlled, surface-controlled (adsorption) process, or a combination of both [78].

where i is the current response, v is the scan rate, a and b are variable parameters. When the b value, obtained from the slope, is 0.5, the charge transfer is the diffusion-controlled process. However, if the b value is 1, it is instead the surface-controlled process. As shown in Fig. 9(a), in the absence of CDs the charge transfer in H3PO4/rice electrolyte was predominantly the diffusion-controlled process. An addition to the CDs to the H3PO4/rice electrolyte, however, increased the b value from 0.610 to 0.746, indicating that the CDs improved the surface-controlled process of charge transfer. A b value of 0.746 also confirms that both diffusion- and surface-controlled processes virtually equally contributed to the charge transfer. A contribution from both surface- and diffusion-controlled processes might synergistically lead to optimum charge transfer efficiency. To further confirm this finding, the adsorption equation (Eq. 7) [79] and Randles-Sevcik diffusion equation (Eq. 8) [80] were used to further analyze the charge transfer process.

Therefore, it can be concluded that both adsorption- and diffusion-controlled processes contributed to the charge transfer. Interestingly, it is obvious that the slopes of H3PO4/rice/0.05%wt CDs from both plots are higher than those of H3PO4/rice. This verifies that the addition of CDs in electrolyte enhanced key parameters, such as number of transferred electrons (n), surface coverage of the adsorbed species ( Γ ) and/or diffusion coefficient of the electroactive species (D
0 ), as these are parts of the slopes. The improvement in these parameters is in a good agreement with the ability of the negatively charged CDs as electrolytic ions and ion facilitators [81]. Furthermore, the CDs as pseudocapacitive materials due to the presence of nitrogen and oxygen atoms on the surface can improve the pseudocapacitance of the supercapacitors [82]. It can therefore be concluded that the CDs led to a distinct improvement in several factors for charge transfer via diffusion and adsorption processes, which in turn reduced the series resistance and enhanced the specific capacitance of the supercapacitors.

As the CV curves are quasi-rectangular in shape, our supercapacitors are not perfect EDL capacitors. Pseudocapacitance also contributed to the specific capacitance. For further investigation, the Dunn’s method was used to examine the contribution from surface capacitance (EDL) and pseudocapacitance (diffusion) [83,84]. The relationship between current response (i(v)) at a fixed potential and a square root of scan rate is shown in Eqs. (9) and (10).

As shown in Fig. 10(a), upon the addition of CDs, the contribution from surface capacitance became more pronounced at all scan rates, consistent with analysis using the Cottrell’s equations. As compared to only 28% of H3PO4/rice system (Fig. 10b), the surface capacitance contributes 69% in the H3PO4/rice/0.05%wt CDs electrolyte system at a scan rate of 30 mV s−1 (Fig. 10c). It can be concluded that 1) the CDs improved the surface capacitance contribution (EDL), 2) both surface- and diffusion-controlled capacitances equally played an important role in controlling the supercapacitor performance, and 3) the CDs synergistically led to optimal contributions from both surface and diffusion processes. Furthermore, they also acted as ion-transport facilitators and pseudocapacitive materials, enhancing several key factors necessary for charge storage in supercapacitors. We also believe that the unique characteristics of CDs with nanometer size, surface functional groups, surface charge, and extraordinary hydrophilicity possibly provided electro-active properties and improved accessibility and wettability between electrode and electrolyte to enhance surface adsorption and diffusion of ions and to reduce the series resistance, thereby leading to the enhanced electrical performance of the supercapacitors (Fig. 10d).

Next, the recyclability of our supercapacitor was investigated. It was found that the specific capacitance did not decrease for 1000 cycles and then slowly dropped to 84% after 5000 cycles ( Fig. 11a). We also investigated with the shelf life of our H3PO4/rice/0.05%wt CDs electrolyte. Photos of electrolytes were taken of and CV were measured over a period of 8 weeks (Fig. 11b and c). The photos showed no changes of the electrolytes. They still looked like quasi-solid over 8 weeks. The specific capacitance did not significantly vary as a function of time. We believed that H3PO4 acted as a preservative to prevent rice from spoiling. These results confirm that our supercapacitor can be used for several thousand cycles and a long period of time, which will be sufficient for practical applications.

In this work, a novel approach for fabricating an all-in-one, quasi-solid supercapacitor from bio-renewable rice was demonstrated. Specifically, activated carbon and CDs were prepared from a single source: rice straw in one pot, whereas the electrolyte was prepared from rice grain. CDs were demonstrated to enhance the electrical performance through the introduction of electro-active species that can reduce series resistance and improve surface wettability. The highest specific capacitance and potential window were 491 F g−1 and 2.4 V, yielding an energy density of 98 Wh kg−1. Based on these results, the use of rice straw-derived activated carbon electrode and H3PO4/rice electrolytes with CDs as additives produced a supercapacitor with one of the best performance reported to date. Intensive electrochemical analysis indicates that the CDs enhanced the supercapacitor performance by reducing the series resistance via the improvement in number of transferred electrons, surface coverage of the adsorbed species and/or diffusion coefficients of the electroactive species. More importantly, we have turned biomass in rice straw and bio-renewable source in rice into a high-performance supercapacitor. The development of environmentally friendly, high-performance supercapacitors in this work will pave the way for the practical, large-scale fabrication of energy storage systems for a range of electronic devices and also the soon-to-be-popular portable and wearable electronics. This also work provides an approach that will simultaneously reduce the amount of agricultural waste, value-add the waste, and lower the cost of supercapacitors. Furthermore, the all-in-one strategy developed here will pave the way for other potential bio-renewable sources to be used for the fabrication of, but not limited to, supercapacitors.
